# Air Pressure & Respiratory Data

<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

Supporting data for the French Horn Effort Rubric. All claims cite sources in [citations.md](../citations.md).

---

## Clarinet Blowing Pressure

### Primary Source: Pamies-Vila et al. 2018 [5]

Study of N=11 clarinettists (2 professional, 9 advanced students, mean age 23). German Bb clarinet (Thomann GCL-416), synthetic Legere reed (strength 2.5). Chalumeau register only. Mouth pressure measured via piezo-resistive transducer at 50 kHz.

**Mouth pressure (DC component):**

| Condition | Mean (kPa) | SD (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Piano (all articulations, both tempi) | 2.7 | 0.3 |
| Forte, slow tempo (60 bpm) | 3.8 | 0.6 |
| Forte, fast tempo (120 bpm) | 4.2 | 0.6 |

**Mouthpiece pressure envelope (AC oscillation amplitude):**

| Dynamic | Mean (kPa) | SD (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Piano | 1.7 | 0.2 |
| Forte | 2.6 | 0.4 |

**Statistical significance:**
- Dynamics effect on mouth pressure: F(1,9) = 62.22, p < 0.001, partial η² = 0.87
- Tempo effect: F(1,9) = 33.88, p < 0.001, partial η² = 0.79
- No significant articulation effect on mouth pressure

**Important:** The highest mean pressure reported in this study was 4.2 kPa (forte, fast tempo). No "6.5-7.5 kPa maximum mouthpiece pressure" was found in this paper.

### Secondary Source: Bouhuys 1965/1968 (cited in secondary literature)

Clarinet low tones: 2.5-3.9 kPa. This is consistent with the Pamies-Vila data [5].

---

## Brass Instrument Blowing Pressure

### Trumpet (Bouhuys 1965, cited in secondary sources)

| Dynamic | Pressure (kPa) |
|---|---|
| Pianissimo (pp) | ~4 |
| Fortissimo (ff) | ~14 |
| Extreme high register | ~10 (from secondary conversion) |

Going up one octave approximately doubles intrathoracic pressure.

### Tuba

- High notes: 60-70 mmHg (~8.0-9.3 kPa) — cited from Elghozi et al. 2008 in Bouros 2018 [6]. This is not original data from [6].
- General playing: very low pressure, 1-10 ounces of mercury (~0.2-2.1 kPa approximately) — from secondary web sources.
- Flow rates: 7 L/min (~0.12 L/s, softest) to >120 L/min (~2 L/s, full volume) — from secondary web sources.

### French Horn — Limited Data Available

Quantitative routine-playing pressure data for French horn is sparse in the accessible peer-reviewed literature. Available information:

- Classified in the "high pressure, low flow" category alongside trumpet (secondary sources).
- Maximum blowing pressure cited as up to 25 kPa for experienced players at extreme range (from ResearchGate study on mouthpiece lip-pressing force — unverified).
- Threshold blowing pressure approximately proportional to frequency of note played.
- Sound output rises ~15 dB per doubling of blowing pressure in the extended region, ~3 dB in the saturation region.
- Conical bore (vs. trumpet's cylindrical bore) reduces back-pressure somewhat, meaning the player may perceive less resistance than trumpet at equivalent notes.

**Estimated French horn routine-playing range: 3-10+ kPa** (extrapolated from trumpet data, adjusted for bore differences). This estimate is constructed, not directly measured in any source we accessed. The lower bound (3 kPa) reflects that horn plays in a similar range to trumpet but with less resistance; the upper bound reflects high-register demands similar to trumpet.

---

## General Wind Instrument Ranges [7]

From UNSW Physics (Joe Wolfe):

| Parameter | Range |
|---|---|
| Blowing pressure (all wind instruments) | 1-10 kPa |
| Airflow rate (all wind instruments) | 0.1-1 L/s |
| Air speed through mouth/throat (~3 cm² cross-section) | 0.3-3 m/s |
| Air speed at lip aperture (~0.1 cm² cross-section) | Up to 100 m/s |

**Clarinet-specific note from [7]:** Unlike most instruments, clarinet's higher pitch does not require higher pressure at the same loudness. This is a unique aerodynamic property of the clarinet's bore/reed system.

Relationship: 1 kPa pressure difference produces roughly 40 m/s air speed; 4 kPa produces about 80 m/s [7].

---

## Respiratory Function: Brass vs. Woodwind [6]

### Spirometry Data (Bouros et al. 2018)

N=32 professional wind instrumentalists (16 brass: 7 trumpet, 4 trombone, 4 tuba, 1 horn; 16 woodwind: 9 clarinet, 5 saxophone, 2 flute). Pre- and post-rehearsal spirometry after 90-minute rehearsal.

**All participants (mean ± SD):**

| Parameter | Pre-rehearsal | Post-rehearsal | p-value |
|---|---|---|---|
| FEV1 (L) | 3.85 ± 1.26 | 3.78 ± 1.09 | 0.402 |
| FVC (L) | 3.47 ± 1.05 | 3.46 ± 0.91 | 0.958 |
| FEV1/FVC (%) | 90.67 ± 9.58 | 93.60 ± 5.35 | 0.065 |
| VC (L) | 4.21 ± 1.11 | 4.13 ± 1.03 | 0.307 |

**Brass vs. woodwind comparison:**
- Wooden instrument players had FVC 0.49 L lower than brass
- Wooden instrument players had FEV1 0.7 L lower than brass
- Wooden instrument players had VC 0.58 L lower than brass
- Woodwind players showed significant improvement in FEV1/FVC%: from 89.54% to 93.82% (p = 0.030)

**Interpretation:** Brass players tend to have higher lung capacity measures than woodwind players. This may reflect self-selection (players with larger lung capacity gravitate to brass) and/or training adaptation over time. The study found no significant change in respiratory volumes after a 90-minute rehearsal, suggesting that playing does not acutely deplete respiratory capacity [6].

**Key quote from [6]:** "Wind instrument players do not have a VC greater than their predicted age, height, weight and gender."

---

## Air Velocity at Distance [8]

From Spahn et al. 2021 (N=14 professional players, Bamberg Symphony). Measured air velocity escaping from instruments at 1 m, 1.5 m, and 2 m distance.

| Instrument | Air velocity at 1 m |
|---|---|
| Horn | <0.1 m/s |
| Clarinet | <0.1 m/s |
| Tuba | 0.13 m/s |
| Oboe | 0.15 m/s |
| Background room air | ~1 m/s |

"No airflows escaping from any of the wind instruments were measurable beyond a distance of 1.5 m" [8].

**Relevance to rubric:** Both horn and clarinet produce negligible air output at distance. This confirms that air is being used for sound production (vibrating the reed/lips) rather than being blown through the instrument as bulk flow. The effort goes into maintaining sustained mouth pressure, not into volumetric airflow.

---

## Clarinet vs. French Horn — Pressure Comparison Summary

| Parameter | Clarinet | French Horn (est.) | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Routine playing pressure (piano-mf) | 2.7-3.8 kPa [5] (mf ~3.3 kPa is interpolated) | 3-6 kPa (est.) | 1.1-1.6× |
| Loud playing pressure (forte-ff) | 3.8-4.2 kPa [5] | 6-10+ kPa (est.) | 1.4-2.4× |
| Airflow rate | 0.1-1 L/s [7] | 0.1-1 L/s [7] | ~1× |
| Air velocity at 1 m distance | <0.1 m/s [8] | <0.1 m/s [8] | ~1× |

**Key finding:** The horn requires moderately higher sustained mouth pressure than the clarinet (estimated 1.1-2.4× depending on register and dynamics) but similar volumetric airflow. The primary breath challenge in switching from clarinet to horn is maintaining higher pressure for longer durations, not producing more air volume.

---

## Unit Conversions

| Unit | Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 1 kPa | 10.05 cmH₂O |
| 1 kPa | 7.50 mmHg |
| 1 mmHg | 0.1333 kPa |
| 1 cmH₂O | 0.0981 kPa |

---

## Caveats

- French horn blowing pressure data is sparse in the accessible peer-reviewed literature. The estimated ranges above are extrapolated from trumpet data and general brass acoustics principles, not direct horn measurements. This is explicitly noted wherever estimates appear.
- The Pamies-Vila study [5] used only the chalumeau register. Higher registers (clarion, altissimo) may require different pressures, though [7] notes clarinet is unusual in not requiring higher pressure for higher pitch at the same loudness.
- Bouros 2018 [6] is a spirometry study — it contains no original blowing pressure measurements. Pressure values it mentions are citations from other papers.
- Spahn 2021 [8] measured air velocity at distance, not blowing pressure.
